content:
A follow-up to Part 1 (EN on LinkedIn · RU on Habr), where we stood up a two-tier PKI: a Root CA and three intermediate CAs — Person, Server and Code. At the end of Part 1 I promised we'd learn to revoke certificates and run OCSP. That's what we'll do here.

Like Part 1, this article is meant as a hands-on manual: for every command and extension we touch, there's an extended reference of the parameters you can actually use — with syntax, allowed values, defaults and gotchas. If you don't need a given option right now, just skim past the table; it's there so you don't have to dig through man later. Each section has the same shape: first the working commands for the common case, then the full parameter reference.

Tested on versions. Flag names, defaults and extension syntax were verified against the official documentation of OpenSSL master, plus nginx and Apache mod_ssl. OpenSSL evolves per branch: anything marked "OpenSSL 4.0 / master" (for example the nonss qualifier on authorityKeyIdentifier) is not yet available in the stable 3.x line. If you're on OpenSSL 3.0–3.6, double-check the disputed options with openssl <cmd> --help or your version's man before copy-pasting config. The numeric openssl verify error codes above 40 also shifted between branches — confirm them against your version's header.

In this part:

• How a revoked certificate differs from an expired one, and why we need two mechanisms — CRL and OCSP.

• Adding the distribution points (CDP) and AIA to the config so issued certificates "tell" verifiers where to check them.

• Revoking a certificate and working with the CA database.

• Generating a CRL and inspecting it with openssl crl.

• Checking revocation with openssl verify.

• Running an OCSP responder: issuing its certificate, starting the daemon, querying status.

• Publishing the CRL and OCSP over HTTP (nginx), configuring OCSP stapling and revocation checking on the web server.

All paths, file names and config sections are the same as in Part 1. Where you named things differently, substitute your own values.

A bit of theory: CRL vs OCSP

Validity period (notAfter) and revocation are two different things. A certificate can be valid by date yet compromised: an employee left, a private key leaked, an organization changed. The date won't help here — you need a mechanism for the CA to tell the world "don't trust this certificate anymore, even though it hasn't expired."

There are two such mechanisms:

•
CRL (Certificate Revocation List) — a CA-signed list of serial numbers of revoked certificates. The client downloads the whole file and checks whether the serial is in it. Simple, works offline (you can cache it), but the list grows and isn't updated instantly — a CRL has a nextUpdate, until which the client may keep using an old copy.

•
OCSP (Online Certificate Status Protocol) — the client asks a responder about the status of one specific certificate by its serial number and gets a signed good / revoked / unknown answer. Fresher and lighter for the client, but it needs an online service to be reachable.

In practice you ship both: the certificate carries the CRL address (the crlDistributionPoints extension, CDP) and the OCSP address (the authorityInfoAccess extension, AIA). The client decides which to use.

One thing that will save you half an hour of confusion: the CDP and AIA extensions only land in certificates issued after you add them to the config. Previously issued User1, certservice.info, etc. don't carry these links inside — you'll have to re-issue them if you want clients to find the check points automatically. So we start with the config.

Step 1. Add CDP and AIA to the intermediate CA configs

In Part 1 the configs already had the [ crl_ext ] (for the CRL itself) and [ ocsp ] (for the future responder certificate) sections prepared, but the end-entity certificate extensions carried no links to where to check them. Let's add them.

Open /root/ca/config/PersonIntermediateCA.cnf and, in the section you use to sign end-entity certificates (in Part 1 that's user_cert for users; server_cert and code_cert for servers and code), add two lines:

[ user_cert ]
# ... whatever was already there from Part 1 ...
basicConstraints = CA:FALSE
keyUsage = critical, nonRepudiation, digitalSignature, keyEncipherment
extendedKeyUsage = clientAuth, emailProtection
# --- new in Part 2: where the client goes to check revocation status ---
crlDistributionPoints = URI:http://pki.certservice.info/crl/PersonIntermediateCA.crl
authorityInfoAccess = OCSP;URI:http://ocsp.certservice.info/

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

A few syntax subtleties, all visible in the reference below:

• In authorityInfoAccess the separator between the method and the address is a semicolon (OCSP;URI:...), and the separator between entries is a comma. A common mistake is to put a comma instead of the ;.

•
crlDistributionPoints has two forms. The short one — one or more URIs right in the value, comma-separated:

crlDistributionPoints = URI:http://pki.certservice.info/crl/PersonIntermediateCA.crl, URI:http://pki2.certservice.info/crl/PersonIntermediateCA.crl

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

The expanded one — when you need reasons or CRLIssuer: a reference to one section by name, and crucially without the @ prefix (this is a CDP quirk; for subjectAltName/issuingDistributionPoint, on the contrary, you write @section):

crlDistributionPoints = cdp_person
[ cdp_person ]
fullname = URI:http://pki.certservice.info/crl/PersonIntermediateCA.crl
reasons = keyCompromise, CACompromise

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

⚠️ The form crlDistributionPoints = @cdp1, @cdp2 (a list of @-sections) does not work as expected for CDP. If you really need several reason-scoped DistributionPoints, verify the syntax on your OpenSSL version (openssl req -text after generation) or build the extension via raw/DER encoding.

• AIA with several entries (plus the address to download the issuer certificate itself, caIssuers) is conveniently spelled out via a section with indices:

authorityInfoAccess = @aia
[ aia ]
OCSP;URI.0 = http://ocsp.certservice.info/
caIssuers;URI.0 = http://pki.certservice.info/certs/PersonIntermediateCA.cert.pem

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

•
Do not mark CDP and AIA as critical. Per RFC 5280 these extensions must be non-critical — otherwise old clients that can't process them will reject the whole certificate. critical is appropriate for issuingDistributionPoint (but that lives in the CRL, not in the certificate).

Do the same in [ server_cert ] and [ code_cert ] (and in ServerIntermediateCA.cnf / CodeIntermediateCA.cnf, changing the CRL file name to ServerIntermediateCA.crl and CodeIntermediateCA.crl).

After editing the configs, re-issue the end-entity certificates that need these links (the procedure from Part 1). To check that the extensions landed in the certificate:

openssl x509 -in /root/ca/PersonIntermediateCA/certs/User1.cert.pem \
-noout -text | grep -A2 -E "CRL Distribution|Authority Information"

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

We expect X509v3 CRL Distribution Points with our URI and Authority Information Access: OCSP - URI:....

Extended reference: X.509v3 extensions for revocation (CDP, AIA, IDP, Key Identifiers)

All extensions and fields that define CRL distribution points and OCSP access. Documentation section — man5/x509v3_config.

Parameter
Syntax / values
Default
Description & notes

crlDistributionPoints
crlDistributionPoints = URI:http://example.com/myca.crl | crlDistributionPoints = cdp_section (section name without @)
values: short form: GeneralName(s) separated by commas (e.g. URI:...); or a section name (without @) with fields fullname/relativename/CRLIssuer/reasons
—
CRL Distribution Points extension: specifies where to download the certificate revocation list (CRL) from. Supports a short form (one or more GeneralName values separated by commas) and an expanded form via a section with fields fullname, relativename, CRLIssuer, reasons.
⚠️ In the short form (name-value), a single DistributionPoint is built, where the value becomes the fullName field, and the reasons and CRLIssuer fields are omitted. For multiple DistributionPoint values with reasons/CRLIssuer, the expanded form via section(s) is required. A common mistake is to forget the scheme (URI:) and supply a bare URL.

fullname
fullname = URI:http://example.com/myca.crl
values: GeneralName in subject alternative name format (URI:, dirName:, etc.)
—
DistributionPoint section field (for crlDistributionPoints and issuingDistributionPoint): the full name of the CRL distribution point. Has the same format as subject alternative name.
⚠️ In the man page it is written in lowercase: fullname. Mutually exclusive with relativename within a single DistributionPoint — specify either fullname or relativename, but not both.

relativename
relativename = section (value — a DN fragment, given as a DN section)
values: a distinguished name fragment (RDN), given via a section in DN format
—
DistributionPoint section field: the name of the distribution point given as a distinguished name fragment; set as the value of the nameRelativeToCRLIssuer field.
⚠️ The value is taken as a DN fragment (same format as a distinguished name). Mutually exclusive with fullname.

CRLIssuer
CRLIssuer = dirName:issuer_sect
values: GeneralName (usually dirName:section) in subject alternative name format
—
DistributionPoint section field: the name of the CRL issuer when the CRL is signed by an entity other than the certificate issuer itself (indirect CRL). The value is in the same format as subject alternative name.
⚠️ Different versions of the OpenSSL documentation use different casing for the field name (CRLIssuer and CRLissuer); after issuance verify with openssl x509 -text that the CRL Issuer made it into the extension. Used for indirect CRLs, often together with dirName: pointing to a section with C/O/CN.

reasons
reasons = keyCompromise, CACompromise
values: keyCompromise, CACompromise, affiliationChanged, superseded, cessationOfOperation, certificateHold, privilegeWithdrawn, AACompromise
—
DistributionPoint section field: a multi-valued field containing the revocation reasons (reason flags) covered by this CRL distribution point. Listed separated by commas.
⚠️ The exact set of recognized values from the man page: keyCompromise, CACompromise, affiliationChanged, superseded, cessationOfOperation, certificateHold, privilegeWithdrawn, AACompromise. Casing is critical: keyCompromise and CACompromise are written exactly like this. removeFromCRL is not included in this list.

keyCompromise
reasons = keyCompromise
—
Value of the reasons / onlysomereasons field: compromise of the subject's private key.
⚠️ One of the recognized reason flag values. Casing matters.

CACompromise
reasons = CACompromise
—
Value of the reasons / onlysomereasons field: compromise of the certification authority (CA) key.
⚠️ Written with uppercase CA: CACompromise.

affiliationChanged
reasons = affiliationChanged
—
Value of the reasons / onlysomereasons field: change of the subject's affiliation (for example, a change of organization).
⚠️ One of the recognized reason flag values.

superseded
reasons = superseded
—
Value of the reasons / onlysomereasons field: the certificate has been replaced by a new one.
⚠️ One of the recognized reason flag values.

cessationOfOperation
reasons = cessationOfOperation
—
Value of the reasons / onlysomereasons field: cessation of operation/use of the certificate.
⚠️ One of the recognized reason flag values.

certificateHold
reasons = certificateHold
—
Value of the reasons / onlysomereasons field: temporary suspension of the certificate (hold).
⚠️ One of the recognized reason flag values.

privilegeWithdrawn
reasons = privilegeWithdrawn
—
Value of the reasons / onlysomereasons field: withdrawal of the subject's privileges/authority.
⚠️ One of the recognized reason flag values.

AACompr
